access.accessMethod: Checked the current Discovery record, which explicitly reports that the item is not digitised; no public complete manuscript image object was found.
access.entityLabel: The National Archives E 164/26

access.accessMethod: Obtain or bring a valid reader's ticket and book a standard reading-room visit at Kew, then order E 164/26 under current document-ordering rules.
access.entityLabel: The National Archives E 164/26

access.accessMethod: Ask first whether TNA holds an existing complete surrogate or can reproduce the complete E 164/26 host, including covers or boards, spine, clasp, pastedown(s), endleaves, blanks, every recto and verso, the unfoliated Mirror section, English register ff. 1-61, blank ff. 61v-65v, and later Latin section ff. 66r-112v. If complete-host coverage is infeasible, use a page check to order the broadest approved section/folio set and preserve every boundary explicitly.
access.entityLabel: The National Archives E 164/26
access.format: Digital or paper copies subject to item condition, page check, provider capacity, and rights clearance
notes: Current public guidance lists a £9.92 page-check fee and approximately 29 working days as of 2026-07-16; fees and turnaround can change.
Clark's published fols. 1-61 boundary excludes the unfoliated Mirror fragment unless it is named separately in the request.
Section- or folio-level ordering is a fallback after the complete-host/existing-surrogate question, not the primary preservation goal.
A request route is not proof that a complete-host reproduction will be approved.

rights.basis: TNA says record images are outside the Open Government Licence and that publication or reuse may require permission; the physical record's legal status does not settle image rights.
rights.component: Copies or images ordered from The National Archives

entities.description: A fifteenth-sixteenth-century composite codex held at Kew. Clark distinguishes three abruptly bounded sections: (A) an unfoliated title and partial chapter table of Nicholas Love's Mirror, copied about 1450; (B) the English Oseney register, copied or translated about 1460 and numbered ff. 1-61, with text ending on f. 61 and ff. 61v-65v blank; and (C) later Latin papal confirmations at ff. 66r-112v, after 1513, outside the edited target.

holding.notes: The Discovery record says the record is open, held at The National Archives, Kew, and not digitised. Its edition note explicitly limits Clark's edition to fols. 1-61.

entities.label: The National Archives E 164/26

physicalDescription.extent: Composite host through at least f. 112v; Clark target limited to the prefixed fragment and ff. 1-61
notes: All three principal sections end abruptly.
The full-host boundary matters for reproduction requests: ordering only fols. 1-61 omits the Mirror fragment, while ordering the entire host includes the out-of-scope Latin section.
physicalDescription.support: Composite paper-and-vellum quires: bundles of four paper sheets laid inside one vellum sheet; stamped leather binding on boards with clasp remains and a vellum pastedown

rights.basis: Copyright status is not used to characterize physical custody or reading-room access. The Discovery record's Public Record(s) legal status does not by itself establish copyright or reuse status for manuscript content or modern images.
rights.component: Physical manuscript as a material object and custody/access layer

rights.basis: TNA says record images are outside the Open Government Licence and that publication or reuse may require permission; the physical record's legal status does not settle image rights.
rights.component: Copies or images ordered from The National Archives

access.format: Physical manuscript consultation by advance appointment

access.accessMethod: Ask first whether an existing complete surrogate or complete-host reproduction can be supplied for D&C vi.a.1 (= MS 343), including covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, endleaves, blanks, and every recto/verso of all i + 353 folios. If complete coverage is infeasible, request the broadest approved research-image set and map the English source relation to cartulary ff. 9r-about 93v within the full cartulary at ff. 9r-343v.
access.entityLabel: Christ Church D&C vi.a.1 (= MS 343)
access.format: Existing images or approved research photography, if available
notes: Current guidance says Christ Church is not accepting new high-resolution photography requests; an enquiry may still establish whether existing images or research-use options are available.
Requestability is therefore narrower than a guaranteed new complete high-resolution reproduction service.
Exact-folio ordering is a fallback after the complete-host/existing-surrogate question, not the primary preservation goal.

rights.basis: Current imaging guidance requires direct enquiry and supplies no item-level open license for D&C vi.a.1; permission and fees, if any, are provider-specific.
rights.component: Existing images or research photography supplied by Christ Church

dateStatements.label: principal production
dateStatements.value: late thirteenth century, perhaps 1280-1284, with additions through the sixteenth century

entities.description: The second Latin Oseney cartulary and Clark's stated exemplar for the Middle English translation. The cartulary occupies ff. 9r-343v; the English fragment follows its order through approximately f. 93v, with one or two English deeds absent from the surviving Latin text.

holding.notes: Current Christ Church and MARCO designation supplied alongside Clark's historical short form.

entities.label: Christ Church D&C vi.a.1 (= MS 343)

notes: Produced at Oxford in several hands.
The aggregate manuscript contains Latin, Anglo-Norman, and Middle English, but the source register translated in E 164/26 is Latin.
The current catalog says William of Sutton, abbot 1267-1284, composed and ordered the cartulary; charters extend to 1280 and later blank spaces received additions through 1474.

rights.basis: Copyright status is not used to characterize physical custody or appointment-based access; no item-level public facsimile license was found.

entities.description: A modern scholarly working intermediary used by Clark. Clark credits Salter's own transcript of the Latin register and says nearly every page and line benefited from it; Salter also communicated selected Cotton readings. Its present survival, extent, and location were not established in public sources.
entities.label: H. E. Salter's working transcript and collation of the Oseney Latin register
notes: The provisional catalog_description type follows repository precedent for a modern working transcript intermediary and prevents treating Salter's material as a second medieval manuscript witness.
This entity preserves the documented material intermediary instead of asserting direct Clark autopsy of the Christ Church and Cotton manuscripts.
The token-level allocation of Christ Church and Cotton readings within Salter's working material remains unresolved.

next_steps: Obtain images of the prefixed unfoliated section and collate every heading against current Mirror manuscript inventories.
Keep Clark's Bonaventure and John Morton shorthand as edition history, not preferred modern authorship.
openQuestions.question: Which textual recension and manuscript family of Nicholas Love's Mirror does the E 164/26 title and partial chapter table represent?

next_steps: Ask first for an existing complete surrogate or complete-host images covering binding surfaces, pastedown(s), endleaves, blanks, every recto/verso, and all three sections through at least f. 112v.

If complete-host coverage is infeasible, inspect or order the broadest approved fallback covering the unfoliated prefixed section and English register ff. 1r-61r while retaining blank ff. 61v-65v and later Latin ff. 66r-112v as host-boundary context.
Do not promote the 279 DIV3 units to independent physical deeds or separately cataloged works without direct comparison.
openQuestions.question: How do Clark's 279 documentary/editorial entry units and 33 title groups map to physical deed boundaries in E 164/26, and does the missing XXXIII represent lost content or only a dropped numeral?

next_steps: Submit an enquiry quoting E 164/26 and ask first about an existing complete surrogate or complete-host capture of covers/boards, spine, clasp, pastedown(s), endleaves, blanks, every recto/verso, the unfoliated Mirror section, English register ff. 1-61, and Latin ff. 66r-112v.
Use a page check and section/folio ordering only as a fallback if complete-host coverage is infeasible, while preserving every boundary explicitly.
Ask separately about condition, completeness, delivery format, publication permission, and image reuse terms before relying on the order route.
notes: No request was submitted during this research; status remains unresolved rather than implying an open external ticket.
openQuestions.question: Will TNA identify an existing complete surrogate or approve complete-host reproduction of E 164/26, including binding surfaces, pastedown(s), endleaves, blanks, every recto/verso, and all three sections through at least f. 112v?

next_steps: Search Salter papers, EETS editorial correspondence, and institutional archival catalogs.
If found, inventory the transcript's extent and collate Clark's notes token by token before asserting direct source allocation.
openQuestions.question: Where, if anywhere, does H. E. Salter's working Latin-register transcript or collation survive, and which exact Clark readings derive from Christ Church versus Cotton?

next_steps: Email Christ Church Library and Archives with the current shelfmark and ask first about an existing complete surrogate or complete-host coverage of all i + 353 folios and binding surfaces.
Ask whether existing images, on-site photography, fees, delivery formats, and reuse permissions are available while new high-resolution orders are suspended.
notes: No enquiry was submitted during this research.
openQuestions.question: Can Christ Church supply existing images or authorize research photography for D&C vi.a.1 (= MS 343), especially the Latin register through approximately f. 93v?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections for written clarification before redistributing the transcription, markup, or cached image bundle.
Keep the medieval works, Clark edition, Internet Archive scans, U-M encoding, U-M page images, and repository artifact as distinct rights components.
openQuestions.question: Does U-M's current No Copyright-United States statement supersede the conditional availability notice embedded in the 2006 XML, and does it cover the current IIIF image derivatives?

reviewNotes: The edition-level composite, two historical components, three physical manuscript witnesses, modern scholarly edition parts, U-M text encoding, U-M image carrier, Internet Archive scans, and repository XML remain separate entities.

TNA E 164/26 is a composite host: section A is only a title/partial contents-table fragment of Nicholas Love's Mirror; section B is the incomplete English register; section C is a later Latin papal-confirmation collection at ff. 66r-112v outside the target.

Christ Church D&C vi.a.1 (= MS 343) is the second Latin register and stated translation exemplar. Cotton MS Vitellius E XV is the earlier first Latin register and contributes only selected comparison readings through Salter's mediation.
Clark's edition groups 279 DIV3 documentary/editorial units under 33 title containers; those units are not automatically 279 separate physical charters or literary works.

Clark's older Bonaventure and John Morton shorthand is not promoted to preferred authorship. Modern evidence ties the fragment to Nicholas Love's translation/adaptation of the pseudo-Bonaventuran Meditationes vitae Christi, whose Latin authorship remains disputed.

Current U-M No Copyright-United States language, the conditional 2006 XML availability notice, and the repository's broad CC BY-SA output statement are preserved as separate, unresolved rights evidence.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a copy or version of University of Michigan electronic item AHA2740.0001.001. U-M selectively encoded Andrew Clark's two-part 1907-1913 EETS edition and linked it to a 322-image composite carrier. Clark's 1907 Part I transcribes two distinct historical components of TNA E 164/26: an unfoliated title and partial chapter-table fragment of Nicholas Love's Mirror, then the Middle English Oseney register at numbered ff. 1r-61r. The register translates the second Latin Oseney cartulary, Christ Church D&C vi.a.1 (= MS 343), through approximately f. 93v. Clark's comparison depended materially on H. E. Salter's working transcript and communications; selected Cotton Vitellius E XV readings do not make that earlier first Latin register the English translation's exemplar. The host's later Latin papal-confirmation section at ff. 66r-112v is outside Clark's target. Complete Clark scans and a complete 322-canvas U-M image bundle are cached; current catalogs expose no public complete facsimile of any of the three physical manuscripts.

catalogSummary.region: The cartulary is associated with Oseney Abbey and Oxfordshire; the independent Mirror fragment is English, but its exact copy origin is unresolved
catalogSummary.title: The English Register of Oseney Abbey

catalogingSubject.label: The English Register of Oseney Abbey
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the edition-level composite represented by U-M item AHA2740 and the repository XML. Its historical text comprises two distinct components in The National Archives E 164/26: an unfoliated title and partial chapter-table fragment of Nicholas Love's Middle English Mirror of the Blessed Life of Jesus Christ, and an incomplete Middle English translation of the Oseney Abbey Latin cartulary now Christ Church D&C vi.a.1 (= MS 343), represented by 279 editorial/documentary entries in 33 title groups. Andrew Clark's 1907 text, his 1913 forewords and indexes, the physical composite host, digital page images, U-M encoding, and repository XML are separate transmission layers described in the linked lineage manifest. The host's later Latin papal-confirmation section at ff. 66r-112v lies outside both Clark's edited target and this metadata subject.

extent.description: Intellectual-item total: two prose historical components, comprising one unnumbered partial Mirror chapter-table fragment and one incomplete English register/documentary compilation. Structural/editorial metrics remain separate: the register has 279 DIV3 entry units under 33 title groups, numbered 1-278 with interpolated 117*A. Those units are not counted as separate intellectual items and are not asserted to be 279 independently bounded physical deeds. Parent/root framing and two modern indexes count zero. The XML contains 9 DIV1, 33 DIV2, 279 DIV3, 322 PB, 121 MILESTONE, 2442 P, no L, and no LG.

formStatements.description: The two counted intellectual components—the partial Mirror chapter-table fragment and the English register compilation—are prose; the 279 DIV3 entry units remain structural/editorial metrics rather than separately counted items. The XML contains no verse-line or verse-group elements.

summaries.value: An edition-level composite preserving two distinct Middle English prose components from TNA E 164/26: a title and incomplete chapter table from Nicholas Love's Mirror, and a fragmentary translation of Oseney Abbey's Latin cartulary. The register compilation contains charters and related records on the abbey's foundation, privileges, waterways, chapels, and Oxfordshire estates; Clark organizes its 279 editorial entry units under 33 title groups.

AHA2740 encodes selected matter from Clark's two-volume edition of the composite TNA E 164/26 source. The historical target begins with an independent, abruptly ending title and chapter-table fragment of Nicholas Love's Mirror and continues with an incomplete English translation of the Oseney cartulary. Clark's 279 register entry containers are grouped beneath 33 title headings; they are documentary/editorial units, not 279 separately cataloged literary works.

The Mirror fragment supplies a title and chapter headings through an incomplete thirtieth chapter heading. The register translates charters and related records concerning Oseney Abbey's foundation, transfer of St George's, meadows, waterways, chapels, and estates across Oxfordshire, ending in the Barford group during entry 278. Clark's modern persons/places/matters and words/phrases indexes follow in the encoded back matter.

notes: This established edition title names the encoded composite; it does not collapse the prefixed Mirror fragment and the translated register into one medieval authored work.
titles.value: The English Register of Oseney Abbey

notes: The spelling follows Clark's heading. The encoded portion contains only a title and incomplete chapter table, not the Mirror's continuous text.

The register compilation contains charters and related records on the abbey's foundation, privileges, waterways, chapels, and Oxfordshire estates; Clark organizes its 279 editorial entry units under 33 title groups.
